Distinct from `ATTACKS` (generic physical violence; not necessarily sustained or systematic), `ASSAULTS` (sexual violence specifically), `EXECUTES` (formal death sentence), and the `REVEALS_TO=under_torture` qualifier (which captures the informational *effect* of torture, not the act itself). Major narrative thread: Bolton flaying tradition, Mountain at Harrenhal, Qyburn's "studies", the Bloody Mummers, Black Cells. Without this edge, "who has Ramsay tortured?" cannot be graph-answered. Tier-3 (no qualifier; method is sub-typology the narrative captures). | Torturer → Tortured-person | corpus |
| `AGENT_IN` | Acts as the agent/executor of an event — the participant who actually performed the act. Source is the executor; target is the event node. Reification role edge: use when attaching a character or house to a multi-party event hub instead of (or in addition to) a dyadic binary edge. See `VICTIM_IN` (patient), `COMMANDS_IN` (orderer who did not personally execute), `WIELDED_IN` (instrument). | Person/House → Event (`event.*`) | — |
| `VICTIM_IN` | Receives the action of an event as the victim or patient — the participant on whom the act was performed. Source is the recipient; target is the event node. Reification role edge: complement to `AGENT_IN` on the same event hub. | Person/House → Event (`event.*`) | — |
| `WITNESS_IN` | The **observer/perceiver** slot on an event hub — a character whose *load-bearing perception* of a charged event (violence, death, a secret, an atrocity) is itself worth a graph edge, distinct from acting (`AGENT_IN`), suffering (`VICTIM_IN`), ordering (`COMMANDS_IN`), or combatting (`FIGHTS_IN`). **Default to an EDGE** onto the existing event node (the Q2 three-lens consensus, S117): reify the witnessing as its own `event.*` NODE *only* when the act of seeing owns an outgoing causal edge — i.e. the seeing `CAUSES`/`TRIGGERS`/`MOTIVATES` a downstream event (e.g. `bran-witnesses-jaime-and-cersei`, whose seeing causes his fall) — or the witnessed thing has no node of its own. Mechanical test: *does the seeing need an outgoing edge? yes → node; no → edge.* Promotion path: start as an edge, promote to a node later only if a causal edge actually gets drawn. **Text-anchor gate:** emit only when the prose shows the character actually SEES it — present-but-shielded does NOT qualify (Yoren forces Arya's eyes shut at Ned's execution → Arya is NOT a witness; Sansa, who "could not turn her head," IS). **Boundary vs `ATTENDS`:** `ATTENDS` = voluntary audience at a *staged social/ceremonial gathering* (feast, wedding, tourney-as-a-whole, court, coronation); `WITNESS_IN` = perception of a charged/violent/secret incident. Same person can `ATTENDS` the Tourney of the Hand AND `WITNESS_IN` the Loras-vs-Gregor bout within it (different targets). Tier-1 when verbatim-cited. Added Session 117 (2026-06-21); first instances `sansa-stark WITNESS_IN execution-of-eddard-stark`, `arianne-martell WITNESS_IN myrcella-is-maimed-by-darkstar`, `hoster-tully WITNESS_IN battle-of-the-camps`. | Person → Event (`event.*`) | — |

> **Reification role vocabulary note:** `WIELDED_IN` (already in the Possession & Artifacts section, artifact→event) serves the **instrument** role for reified events — no `INSTRUMENT_IN` type is needed. `COMMANDS_IN` (below) serves the **orderer/instigator** role. Together with `AGENT_IN` and `VICTIM_IN`, these four cover the standard **action/instrument** participant slots (agent, patient, orderer, instrument) on an event hub. The **perceiver/observer** slot is a fifth role, `WITNESS_IN` (S117) — epistemic, not action (the perceiver acts on nothing and receives nothing); voluntary audience at a *staged* gathering is `ATTENDS`, not `WITNESS_IN`.

### Knowledge & Information

> **Deprecated (Session 63, 2026-05-21):** `KNOWS` was removed from the active vocabulary. Stage 4 wiki-prose classification ran an 82.3% fallback rate on KNOWS emits — the semantic boundary ("knows of" vs "met once" vs "heard rumor of") is too blurry for prose-derived classification to enforce. Character-knowledge relationships will be derived from a future Pass-1-based chapter co-occurrence + Information Revealed pass. Existing KNOWS edges in the graph (363 Haiku-emit + 21 Sonnet-control-arm) are preserved as historical record; downstream consumers filter deprecated types on read.

| Edge Type | Description | Directionality | Wiki Source |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|-------------|
| `IGNORANT_OF` | Critically lacks information that the reader or other characters have | Person → Information | — |
| `SEEKS` | Pursuing knowledge, artifact, or person | Seeker → Sought | — |
| `REVEALS_TO` | Discloses information to another | Revealer → Recipient (note what was revealed) | — |
| `DECEIVES` | Deliberately misleads | Deceiver → Deceived (note the deception) | — |
| `DECEIVED_BY` | Reverse of `DECEIVES` — emitted on target nodes pointing to deceiver | Deceived → Deceiver | — |
| `HOARDS` | Institution or person suppresses knowledge | Hoarder → Knowledge | — |
| `INVESTIGATES` | Actively trying to learn or prove something | Investigator → Subject | — |
| `TEACHES` | Transmits knowledge or skill (general/casual instruction) | Teacher → Student | — |
| `TUTORS` | Sustained formal one-on-one mentorship — narrower than `TEACHES` (Syrio→Arya water-dancing, Cressen→Stannis childhood, Aemon→Sam ravenry, Septa Mordane→Sansa) | Tutor → Student | — |
| `HEALS` | Medical or maester treatment — restoration of body, not resurrection of the dead (which is `RESURRECTS`). Maester Luwin healing Bran after the fall; Aemon healing Sam; the unnamed septon healing Sandor. **Excludes:** Red Priests reviving the dead (use `RESURRECTS`); Qyburn's reanimation of the Mountain (use `RESURRECTS`) | Healer → Healed | — |
| `AFFLICTED_BY` | Character suffers from a named disease, condition, or magical affliction (living state). Target is `concept.medical`. Distinct from `KILLED_BY` (target = person), `DIED_AT` (location), `DIED_OF` (cause-of-death; this is the living state). Examples: Jorah Mormont/greyscale, Shireen Baratheon/greyscale, Stannis/burns. | Character → Medical | — |
| `DIED_OF` | Character's death was caused by a named disease/condition (post-mortem state). Target is `concept.medical`. Distinct from `KILLED_BY` (person-killer), `DIED_AT` (location), `EXECUTED_WITH` (judicial weapon). Mirrors `AFFLICTED_BY` for the post-mortem state. Examples: Hoster Tully/Spring Sickness, Albin Massey/Shivers, Medrick Manderly/Winter Fever, the Old King Jaehaerys/Great Spring Sickness. | Character → Medical | — |
| `SPIES_ON` | A spy or agent actively surveils, monitors, or gathers intelligence about the target. Distinct from `SERVES` (employment state) and `MANIPULATES` (wrong direction — spy works for handler, not against target). Westeros has structurally-distinct spy networks (Varys's little birds, Littlefinger's network, Qarth's whispers). Tier-3. | Person → Surveilled-person | n/a (Pass 1 corpus + Sonnet no-fit) |
| `INFORMS` | A spy, agent, or informant reports intelligence to their handler or spymaster. Complements `SPIES_ON` (target = person being watched vs. target = handler receiving reports). Distinct from `SERVES` (too generic) and `REVEALS_TO` (one-time disclosure; INFORMS is the ongoing reporting relationship). Tier-3. | Person → Handler/Spymaster | n/a (Pass 1 corpus + Sonnet no-fit) |

### Emotional & Perceptual

| Edge Type | Description | Directionality | Wiki Source |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|-------------|
| `PERCEIVED_AS` | How POV Character X sees Character Y (with characterization notes) | Perceiver → Perceived | — |
| `TRUSTS` | Places confidence in | Truster → Trusted | — |
| `DISTRUSTS` | Lacks confidence in, suspects | Distruster → Distrusted | — |
| `RESPECTS` | Holds in high regard | Respecter → Respected | — |
| `FEARS` | Afraid of (person, faction, or thing) | Fearer → Feared | — |
| `LOVES` | Deep emotional attachment (familial, romantic, or platonic) | Lover → Loved | — |
| `HATES` | Deep enmity or loathing | Hater → Hated | — |
| `MOURNS` | Grieves for (dead person, lost thing) | Mourner → Mourned | — |
| `PROTECTS` | Acts as guardian or defender | Protector → Protected | — |
| `RESENTS` | Harbors bitterness toward | Resenter → Resented | — |
| `COMPANION_OF` | Close personal friendship or camaraderie. Distinct from `ALLIES_WITH` (political alliance), `TRUSTS` (one-direction confidence), `LOVES` (romantic/deep-familial), `RESPECTS` (cold regard). Use when prose explicitly names a friendship ("good friends with", "sworn brothers", "close companion"). Examples: Patrek Mallister & Edmure Tully; Robert & Ned in their youth; Brienne & Pod; Davos & Salladhor Saan. | Symmetric | — |
| `REPUTED_AS` | Collective reputation or general perception attached to a character without a specific perceiver — distinct from `PERCEIVED_AS` which requires a named POV. Target is `concept.*` (e.g., `concept.magic` for "reputed witch", `concept.craft` for "reputed swordsman", `concept.vice` for "reputed drunkard"). Use when prose narrates a public-domain reputation rather than one character's view of another. | Character → Concept | — |
| `ENCOUNTERS` | Plot-significant face-to-face meeting between two characters, anchored by explicit prose staging (verb gate enforced by validator per CRITICAL RULE — see classify prompt Rule 6). Reserved for first meetings, brief road-crossings, set-piece confrontations short of duel/combat where the meeting itself carries narrative weight. Examples: Dunk ↔ Egg at the Ashford inn; Brienne ↔ Randyll Tarly in AFFC; Arya ↔ Sandor on the kingsroad; Jon ↔ Mance at the Frostfangs; Sam ↔ Coldhands beyond the Wall. NOT for co-presence at events (use `LOCATED_AT` / `ATTENDS` / `FIGHTS_IN`), retinue/court presence (`TRAVELS_WITH`), or formal diplomatic engagement (`NEGOTIATES_WITH`). **Coverage scope (Session 63, 2026-05-21):** Stage 4 captures only wiki-prose-staged meetings — biographical-summary register often elides staging verbs even when meetings happened in-text. Comprehensive character-meeting coverage will come from a future book-derived pass; treat wiki ENCOUNTERS as partial-by-design. Tier-3 (no qualifier). | Symmetric (character ↔ character) | — |

### Spatial & Temporal

| Edge Type | Description | Directionality | Wiki Source |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|-------------|
| `LOCATED_AT` | Entity at location (with book/chapter timestamp). Covers both event-at-place (battle location) and person-at-place (witness location). Deprecated synonym `LOCATED_IN` was emitted by an early parser variant; normalize on read. | Entity → Location | Location, Seat |
| `SEAT_OF` | Primary location of a house or faction | Location → House/Faction | Seat, Seats |
| `TRAVELS_TO` | Movement from one location to another | Traveler → Destination (note origin) | — |
| `TRAVELS_WITH` | Co-presence in someone's company, on the road OR in attendance at court/event. Covers both journeying together (Arya/Gendry/Hot Pie; Sam+Gilly+Aemon to Oldtown; Dunk+Egg) AND retinue/court presence (Robett kneeling among Catelyn's welcomers at Winterfell). Distinct from `COMPANION_OF` (sustained friendship), `ALLIES_WITH` (political), `MEMBER_OF` (faction affiliation), `SERVES` (subordination). Tier-3 (no qualifier). | Symmetric (character ↔ character) | — |
| `BORN_AT` | Birthplace | Person → Location | Born |
| `DIED_AT` | Place of death | Person → Location | Died |
| `BURIED_AT` | Place of burial or interment | Person → Location | Buried |
| `IMPRISONED_AT` | Captive's place of confinement during a specific imprisonment (cell, dungeon, sky cell, black cells, tower used as gaol, prison hulk). Distinct from `LOCATED_AT` (general presence — not captivity-marked), `IMPRISONS` (jailer→captive judicial relationship), and `PRISONER_OF` (captive↔captor symmetric state). Examples: Ned/Black Cells; Tyrion/Eyrie sky cell; Jaime/Robb's camp at Riverrun; Davos/Dragonstone dungeon. Tier-3 (no qualifier). | Captive → Location (`place.location`) | — |
| `CONTEMPORARY_WITH` | Events happen simultaneously or overlap in time | Symmetric | — |
| `REGION_OF` | Location belongs to a larger geographic region | Location → Region | Region, Regions |

### Possession & Ownership

| Edge Type | Description | Directionality | Wiki Source |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|-------------|
| `WIELDS` | Currently bears or uses a weapon/artifact. **Target MUST be `object.artifact`** — animals (mounts, ravens, dragons) use `OWNS` or `BONDED_TO`, never `WIELDS`. | Person → Artifact | — |
| `OWNS` | Possesses (broader than wields — castles, ships, animals) | Owner → Owned | Owner, Owners |
| `ANCESTRAL_WEAPON_OF` | Valyrian steel sword or other hereditary weapon of a house | Weapon → House | Ancestral weapon |
| `FORGED_BY` | Creator/smith of an artifact (the person/group who made it). **NOT for material composition** — use `MADE_OF` for substance/material relationships. | Creator → Artifact | — |
| `MADE_OF` | Artifact is composed of a material (Valyrian steel, dragonglass, dragonbone, weirwood, etc.). Distinct from `FORGED_BY` (smith). | Artifact → Material (`object.material`) | — |
| `LOOTED_BY` | Artifact taken by force or conquest from prior holder. Distinct from `OWNS` (steady state). Captures the transactional moment. | Artifact → New holder | — |
| `REFORGED_INTO` | Original artifact materially transformed into a new artifact (or multiple). The original ceases to exist; the new artifact(s) inherit material and lineage. | Original artifact → Resulting artifact | — |
| `GIFTED_TO` | Deliberate voluntary transfer of an artifact from one person to another as gift or honor. Distinct from `OWNS` (state) and `INHERITED_BY` (death-succession). Note giver in qualifier. | Artifact → Recipient | — |
| `INHERITED_BY` | Artifact passed via inheritance from deceased holder to heir. | Artifact → Heir | — |
| `WIELDED_IN` | Artifact was used in a named event (battle, execution, ritual). Distinct from `WIELDS` (person → artifact possession state). Enables artifact-history queries. | Artifact → Event | — |
| `EXECUTED_WITH` | A specific person was executed with a specific weapon (poetic-detail edges: Eddard executed with Ice, etc.). May overlap with `WIELDED_IN` + `EXECUTES`; kept distinct for narrative-precision queries. | Victim → Weapon | — |
| `PURCHASED_FROM` | Transactional acquisition of an artifact (or service) via purchase — distinct from `OWNS` (steady state), `GIFTED_TO` (voluntary transfer), `LOOTED_BY` (taken by force), `INHERITED_BY` (death-succession). Captures the transactional moment + the seller. Examples: Dunk purchasing the dragon-of-Pentos shield from Pate the Old; ship-passage purchases (when concretely named); merchant exchanges that are plot-significant. | Buyer → Seller | — |
| `BUILT` | Character physically built or oversaw the construction of a named structure (castle, tower, sept, wall, monument). Distinct from `FOUNDED` (scoped to organizations/houses/orders) and `OWNS` (steady state). Use when prose explicitly names the builder of a place. Examples: Brandon-the-Builder/The Wall, Brandon-the-Builder/Storm's End (legend), Lord-Triston-Hightower/Starry-Sept. | Builder → Structure (`place.location`) | — |
| `CAPTAIN_OF` | Character is captain (master/commander) of a named vessel. Target MUST be `object.artifact` (the vessel). Distinct from `COMMANDS` (military org) and `OWNS` (ownership state — captains may or may not own the vessel). Examples: Davos Seaworth/Black Betha, Victarion Greyjoy/Iron Victory, Asha Greyjoy/Black Wind, Salladhor Saan/Valyrian. | Captain → Vessel (`object.artifact`) | — |
| `CREW_OF` | Character serves as a crew member (non-captain) of a named vessel — sibling to `CAPTAIN_OF`. Target MUST be `object.artifact` (the vessel). Use when prose explicitly names a non-captain role (oarsman, first mate, ship's cook, sail-master). Captain → use `CAPTAIN_OF`. Generic faction membership → use `MEMBER_OF`. Note specific role in `notes` if known ("first mate"). | Crew member → Vessel (`object.artifact`) | — |

### Identity & Disguise

| Edge Type | Description | Directionality | Wiki Source |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|-------------|
| `ALIAS_OF` | Known by another name. **Substitution test (canonical rule, S86 2026-06-08):** two strings are aliases iff substituting one for the other in any sentence about the entity does NOT change the truth value. "Wedding at the Twins" ↔ "Red Wedding" passes — same event. "Lord Walder calls for the bedding" ↔ "Red Wedding" FAILS — the bedding-call is a moment WITHIN the Red Wedding, not the whole event. Granularity differences are NOT aliases; emit `SUB_BEAT_OF` instead. The dashes-vs-spaces test catches surface variants only, NOT granularity. | Alias → True Identity | Alias, Aliases |
| `DISGUISED_AS` | Actively pretending to be someone/something else | Person → Disguise Identity | — |
| `SAME_AS` | Two references that resolve to the same entity (for cross-identity matching) | Symmetric | — |
| `IMPERSONATES` | Pretending to be a specific other person | Impersonator → Impersonated | — |

### Magic & Supernatural

> These edges capture relationships that involve magical, ritual, or supernatural agency — distinct from in-world physical relations. ASOIAF has rich magical systems (warging, R'hllor blood magic, Faceless-Men identity-transfer, weirwood-bonding, dragon-bonding, resurrection, curse-laying) that infobox extraction cannot reach. These types are *prose-derived only* — the wiki-infobox parser does not emit them.

| Edge Type | Description | Directionality | Wiki Source |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|-------------|
| `WARGS_INTO` | A warg / skinchanger actively occupies the consciousness of an animal or person — e.g., Bran into Summer / Hodor / the heart tree; Arya into Nymeria (dream-skinchange); Varamyr into his wolf / eagle / shadowcat; Orell into his eagle; Jon (low-key) into Ghost | Warg → Vessel | — |
| `BONDED_TO` | Static magical bond between two beings — broader and more permanent than `WARGS_INTO` (which is the active occupation moment). Covers dragon-rider bonds (Daenerys ↔ Drogon, etc.), warg-animal lifelong pairing (Bran ↔ Summer when not actively warging), weirwood-bond (Bran ↔ his three-eyed-crow / weirwood network) | Symmetric | — |
| `SACRIFICES` | Deliberate ritual or magical killing with supernatural/symbolic purpose — distinct from `KILLS` (combat) and `EXECUTES` (judicial). Mirri Maz Duur sacrificing Drogo's life-essence; Daenerys sacrificing her unborn child to magic the dragon eggs; Stannis (via Melisandre) sacrificing Edric Storm's leech-blood / Mance / Penny's brother / (theory-tier) Shireen; Craster sacrificing his sons to the Others | Sacrificer → Victim | — |
| `RESURRECTS` | Returns the dead to life via supernatural means — distinct from `HEALS` (medical), distinct from `KILLED_BY` (semantic reverse). Thoros of Myr resurrects Beric Dondarrion (multiple times); Beric resurrects Catelyn → Lady Stoneheart (ASOS Epilogue); Coldhands resurrected by unknown force (Children?); Patchface drowned-and-returned; Qyburn reanimates the Mountain; Red Priests broadly perform this (Thoros, Moqorro) — `HEALS` is for body-restoration, `RESURRECTS` is for death-reversal | Resurrector → Resurrected | — |
| `CURSES` | A character or magical force lays a curse — Mirri Maz Duur's "when the sun rises in the west" curse on Daenerys; Maggy the Frog's Valonqar prophecy-curse on Cersei; the Curse of Harrenhal (collective); Night's King lore | Curser → Cursed | — |
| `PRACTICES` | Character actively practices a named magical or ritual discipline. Target is `concept.magic` (or `concept.craft` for non-magical-but-named-discipline cases). Distinct from `WARGS_INTO` (active occupation moment), `BONDED_TO` (static pairing), `WORSHIPS` (religious devotion, not the magical practice itself), `CLERGY_OF` (religious office). Examples: Melisandre/shadow-binding, Mirri Maz Duur/maegi-blood-magic, Bran/greendreams, faceless-men/identity-transfer, Qyburn/necromancy. | Character → Magic discipline | — |

### Cultural & Religious

| Edge Type | Description | Directionality | Wiki Source |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|-------------|
| `CULTURE_OF` | Person belongs to a cultural group | Person → Culture | Culture, Race |
| `WORSHIPS` | Follows or serves a deity/religion | Person → Religion | Religion |
| `SACRED_TO` | Location or artifact is holy to a religion | Entity → Religion | — |
| `CLERGY_OF` | Serves as religious official | Person → Religion | — |
| `OFFICIATES` | Character performs the ritual / religious / ceremonial role at a named event (weddings, funerals, coronations, kingsmoots, namedays, knighting ceremonies). Distinct from `CLERGY_OF` (general clergy status, target = religion) and `ATTENDS` (guest/audience). Target is `event.*` or specific named ceremony node. Examples: Melisandre/wedding-of-sigorn-and-alys-karstark; the High Septon/coronation-of-tommen-i; Aeron Damphair/kingsmoot-of-299-ac. | Character → Event | — |
| `HONORED_AT` | Character is the **ceremonial honoree** — the recipient of a conferred honor at a named event (queen of love and beauty crowned at a tourney, a nameday honoree, the dubbed knight at a knighting). The reification-role complement on a ceremonial event hub: the **conferrer** uses `AGENT_IN` (e.g. Rhaegar laying the laurel), the **honoree** uses `HONORED_AT`. Distinct from `ATTENDS` (audience), `OFFICIATES` (performs the rite), `WITNESS_IN` (perceiver), `AGENT_IN` (the one who acts/confers). Added Session 134 (2026-06-23) to retire the off-vocab `CROWNS_QUEEN_OF_LOVE_AND_BEAUTY` tail-LLM leakage; first instance `lyanna-stark HONORED_AT crowning-of-lyanna-at-harrenhal`. Tier-1 when verbatim-cited; no qualifier. | Character → Event (`event.*`) | — |
| `NAMED_AFTER` | An entity was given its name in honor of, or as a reference to, another entity. Captures Westeros's pervasive dynastic name-recycling culture (Rickard Karstark named for Rickard Stark, the many Brandons and Aegons). Distinct from `ALIAS_OF` (a different name for the same entity), `SAME_AS` (identity-resolution), and `DEPICTED_IN` (in-world legend about a person). One-sided: the named entity points to its namesake. Surfaced in Sonnet `no-fitting-type` rejections. Tier-3 (no qualifier). | Entity → Namesake-entity | wiki (dynastic naming patterns) |

### Narrative & Literary

| Edge Type | Description | Directionality | Wiki Source |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|-------------|
| `DEPICTED_IN` | Character is the subject of an in-world text/song/ballad/tale (Danny Flint → "Brave Danny Flint" song, Florian → Florian-and-Jonquil tales). Distinct from `WRITTEN_BY` (author → work). Captures the in-universe legacy/folklore layer. | Character → Text | — |

*continued:*

| Edge Type | Description | Directionality | Wiki Source |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|-------------|
| `FORESHADOWS` | Detail A is a Chekhov's gun for Event B | Detail → Event | — |
| `PARALLELS` | Event/character A mirrors Event/character B thematically | Symmetric | — |
| `SUBVERTS` | Event A inverts the expectation set by B | Subverter → Subverted | — |
| `ECHOES` | Weaker than PARALLELS — structural or verbal similarity without full thematic mirroring | Echo → Source | — |
| `CONTRASTS` | Deliberate opposition in characterization, situation, or outcome | Symmetric | — |
| `WRITTEN_BY` | Authorship of an in-world text (book, song, decree, letter) | Text → Author | Written by |

### Prophecy

| Edge Type | Description | Directionality | Wiki Source |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|-------------|
| `FULFILLS` | Event fulfills prophecy (confirmed) | Event → Prophecy | — |
| `APPEARS_TO_FULFILL` | Possible fulfillment, may be red herring | Event → Prophecy | — |
| `SUBVERTS_PROPHECY` | Contradicts expected fulfillment | Event → Prophecy | — |
| `PROPHESIED_BY` | Who made the prophecy | Prophecy → Prophet | — |
| `SUBJECT_OF_PROPHECY` | Person/event the prophecy is about | Person → Prophecy | — |
| `DREAMS_OF` | In-world prophetic or significant dream/vision about a person, place, event, or symbol. Distinct from `FORESHADOWS` (which is a reader-facing narrative-craft edge); `DREAMS_OF` is character-facing. Heavily concentrated in Bran (greendreams, three-eyed-crow), Daenerys (HotU visions), Jojen, and the Targaryen line (canonical prophetic-dreaming lineage) | Dreamer → Subject | — |

### Evidentiary (Theory Support)

| Edge Type | Description | Directionality | Wiki Source |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|-------------|
| `SUPPORTS` | A real passage the theory community reads as evidence FOR a theory — the edge asserts the reading exists, not that the theory is true. Every edge carries its own byte-verified verbatim quote + chapter:line ref | Evidence node → Theory | — |
| `CONTRADICTS` | A real passage that undermines a theory (or one scoped sub-claim — scope lives in the edge `note`) | Evidence node → Theory | — |
| `CITED_BY` | DEFERRED (S216) — no out-of-world source node type; provenance lives in theory-node frontmatter (`video_sources`, `origin`) | Theory → Source | — |

**Theory-layer conventions (ratified S216–S217, Matt):**

- **Tier floor is absolute:** theory nodes and SUPPORTS/CONTRADICTS edges are **tier 3–5 ALWAYS** — no "event-anchored" or other exceptions. Per-edge tier variance under a node is allowed (a tier-3 node may carry tier-4/5 edges).
- **Layer rule:** facts already owned by tier-1/2 nodes are the theory's *premise* (prose), not theory evidence — don't re-mint them as edges. An existing node CAN source an edge where its confirmed reality bears directly on the claim (the subject-link pattern).
- **Subject-link pattern:** a theory may link to the event/artifact node it is *about* via a scoped evidence edge (GNC G1 precedent: the confirmed event node sources a SUPPORTS into the theory that generalizes it).
- **Parallel-FORESHADOWS (S217):** when a theory-evidence quote foreshadows an event that actually occurs on the page, the factual layer gets a `FORESHADOWS` edge to that event node (canon-visible; tier 2–4 by mapping strength, typically 2–3) alongside the theory-layer SUPPORTS edge. Both edges may carry the same quote — different layers, different claims.
- **Naming:** display names are claim-style ("Roose Bolton is a skin-stealing immortal"); genuinely unresolved multi-candidate whodunits use question-form ("Who is Coldhands?", "Who Wrote the Pink Letter?", "Who is the Hooded Man of Winterfell?").
- **One node per canonical named theory;** umbrella wiki "X/Theories" stubs stay dark unless they ARE the canonical theory.
- **Evidence sources:** any node class whose entity the quote is about may source an evidence edge — including `object.food` (weirwood-paste), legend/`object.text` (rat-cook), and `concept` nodes (wights). From wave 2 onward, theory edges may cite `fab-*` (Fire & Blood) chapters — same quotecheck machinery; chronicler unreliability handled by tier + `in_universe_source`.
- **Ungrounded material** (show-only, TWOW/TWOIAF-fenced, GRRM interviews, community reasoning) is domain-labelled in the node body — never edges.
- **Chat exposure:** theories are invisible to the chat persona (SHARED_RULES guardrail). Plan of record (S217): an off-by-default UI toggle — OFF = runtime tool-layer filter strips theory nodes/edges; ON = labeled-speculation prompt contract. Build is a separate gated session.

### Causal & Plot

| Edge Type | Description | Directionality | Wiki Source |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|-------------|
| `CAUSES` | Event A leads to Event B | Cause → Effect | — |
| `PREVENTS` | Action A blocks Event B | Preventer → Prevented | — |
| `ENABLES` | Condition A makes Event B possible | Enabler → Enabled | — |
| `MOTIVATES` | Event or condition drives a character's actions | Motivation → Actor | — |
| `TRIGGERS` | Immediate cause (narrower than CAUSES — the specific spark) | Trigger → Result | — |
| `SUSPECTED_OF` | A character is suspected — in-world and/or by the reader — of being the agent or cause of an event, where the published text does NOT prove it. The unproven counterpart to `AGENT_IN`/`KILLS`/`CAUSES`: use when the suspicion is itself load-bearing but the act is unconfirmed in the 5 published books. **Capped at Tier-2 — NEVER Tier-1** (Tier-1 is reserved for proven canon; an asserted-as-fact killing uses `KILLS`/`AGENT_IN`). Cite the in-text basis (accusation, portents, timing) in the rationale; out-of-text corroboration (TWOW-preview, author statements) may be noted but does not raise the tier. Added Session 116 (2026-06-20) — first instance: `euron-greyjoy SUSPECTED_OF death-of-balon-greyjoy` (Asha's accusation "Silence returns within a day of my lord father's death" + the storm-portents; Euron's confession exists only in the *Forsaken* TWOW-preview chapter, not published canon, so the killing stays unproven and the node stays `event.death`, not `event.assassination`). | Suspect → Event (`event.*`) | — |

#### The `ENABLES` vs `CAUSES` vs `TRIGGERS` contract (S121, 2026-06-21)

The three forward-causal edges are NOT interchangeable; they form a strength ladder, and the choice is load-bearing for graph honesty (the "sequence-only trap" — drawing causation where only chronology exists). Pick by this rubric:

- **`TRIGGERS`** — A is the **immediate spark** of B; B is the very next beat, nothing decisional sits between them. *Is B the very next thing, or is there a step in between?* If next → TRIGGERS. (`ned-confesses-to-treason TRIGGERS execution-of-eddard-stark`.)

content.text: Let me check the existing p03 extraction to match established conventions and the harvest sidecar format.

content.content:
1	# TMK — Dunk 1
2	
3	## Chapter Metadata
4	- **book:** TMK
5	- **chapter_number:** 1
6	- **pov_character:** Dunk
7	- **pov_chapter_number:** Dunk I
8	- **first_available:** pre-agot
9	- **location_primary:** Whitewalls (Lord Butterwell's castle — the great hall and the wedding feast)
10	- **location_secondary:** The ferry landing and the lake crossing; the inner yard of Whitewalls (squires' tables); (mentioned) Summerhall, Dragonstone, the Red Keep, Lannisport, Casterly Rock/the Rock, the Mandel, the Redgrass Field, Ashford Meadow, the Vale, the Green Fork, Flag's Mire, Crackclaw Point, Highgarden
11	- **approximate_timeline:** "The next day"; unit part 3 of 9. Morning at the ferry crossing gives way to the wedding feast at Whitewalls that same evening. Sequence: the slow ferry crossing and Egg's talk of dragon eggs and Fireball; arrival and seating below the salt at Whitewalls; the wedding procession and toasts; the feast, the tourney talk, the arrival of John the Fiddler, and the wedding pie.
12	- **time_markers:** "The next day"; "each taking more than an hour"; "it would be midday before they were allowed to board the ferry"; Whitewalls "having been raised a mere forty years ago by the grandsire of its present lord"; "The girl was fifteen and freshly flowered, her lord husband fifty and freshly widowed"; "his heir a chinless boy of four"; Frey wants a grandson "before the year is out"; "died in the spring" (the Great Spring Sickness); Bulwer "Past sixty"; "the jousting on the morrow"
13	- **chapter_summary:** The next day Dunk and Egg wait through the slow ferry crossing while Lords Costayne and Shawney quarrel over precedence and the Smallwoods arrive; as they wait, Egg reveals he owns a dragon's egg kept at Summerhall, describes his brothers' eggs and a prophecy that the dragons will return, and recounts the history of Ser Quentyn Ball, called Fireball. Deciding to see the dragon's egg for himself, Dunk brings them to Whitewalls, Lord Butterwell's white-stone castle, where the uninvited hedge knights are seated below the salt for the wedding feast and Egg is turned away to the inner yard. At the bench Dunk meets Ser Uthor Underleaf and learns of the tourney's prizes — the dragon's egg and thirty golden dragons for the loser of the final tilt. Lord Ambrose Butterwell weds his fifteen-year-old Frey bride amid a procession of lords and a round of toasts, during which Ser Glendon Ball spills his cup rather than drink to the Hand, Brynden Rivers. Over the feast the talk turns to tourney competitors; John the Fiddler appears and joins the hedge knights, and a great wedding pie is cut open to loose a hall full of live birds before Dunk excuses himself.
14	- **unit_part:** 3 of 9
15	
16	## Physical Environment
17	- **Weather:** Hot and sweltering at the ferry landing ("nought that Dunk could do but wait and swelter"); midge bites on Dunk's neck
18	- **Season indicators:** Heat and midges; "died in the spring" (the Great Spring Sickness, recalled); the bride "freshly flowered"
19	- **Time of day:** Morning to midday at the ferry crossing; evening for the wedding feast (the bedding to come after)
20	- **Lighting:** The great hall lit for the feast; Butterwell prefers his lord's box "in the shade"; amethysts on the Fiddler's chain "dark"; the pale white marble and gold-veined interior
21	- **Sounds:** Costayne and Shawney's "shouting match over precedence"; a fanfare of trumpets from the minstrel's gallery; "pipes and fiddles filled the air with spritely tunes"; "Laughter rang against the rafters"; squeaks, squawks, and thumps from inside the wedding pie; half a hundred birds bursting forth
22	- **Smells:** The Fiddler "smelled of oranges and limes, with a hint of some strange eastern spice beneath" (nutmeg, perhaps); the ripe white cheese that "smelled as strong as Bennis of the Brown Shield"
23	- **Notable sensory details:** Whitewalls of finely dressed white stone with floors and pillars of milky white marble veined with gold and rafters carved from bone-pale weirwood trunks; the patterned Myrish carpet; the double throne of gilded oak; the two great banners hung from the rafters; the black-bread trencher; the immense brown wedding pie

183	## Events & Actions
184	1. **The ferry crossing** — Ned's ferry is too small for all who wish to cross, so Lords Costayne and Shawney go first with their tails; each of several trips takes more than an hour, slowed by mudflats and by the two lords' shouting match over precedence (Shawney elder, Costayne better born).
185	   - Agent: Ned (ferryman, implied); Lords Costayne and Shawney (cross first)
186	   - Location: The ferry landing and lake
187	   - Outcome: The hedge knights must wait
188	2. **Egg offers to use his boot to go first** — Egg says they could go first "if you let me use my boot"; Dunk refuses, because the lords were there first and are lords.
189	   - Agent: Egg (proposes); Dunk (refuses)
190	   - Patient: Dunk
191	   - Outcome: Refused; the party waits their turn
192	3. **Egg names Costayne and Shawney as former rebels** — Egg says they were "for the Black Dragon" (Shawney, and Costayne's father), describing Costayne's arms and the placement of their banners in Daemon's host; Dunk pinches the boy's lips shut and tells him to hold his tongue.
193	   - Agent: Egg (speaks); Dunk (silences)
194	   - Patient: Egg
195	   - Instrument: Dunk pinching Egg's lips
196	   - Outcome: Egg held his tongue
197	4. **The Smallwoods arrive; the party waits again** — Lord and Lady Smallwood arrive at the landing with their tail, so the hedge knights must wait once more; the "fellowship of the hedge" has dissolved (Glendon aloof, Kyle courting Smallwood, Maynard gossiping with the innkeep).
198	   - Agent: Lord and Lady Smallwood
199	   - Location: The ferry landing
200	   - Outcome: Further delay
201	5. **Dunk warns Egg away from Ser Maynard Plumm** — Something about Plumm troubles Dunk; he warns Egg to stay well away, saying "He could be a robber knight"; the warning only makes Plumm more interesting to Egg.
202	   - Agent: Dunk
203	   - Patient: Egg
204	6. **Egg reveals he owns a dragon's egg** — Egg says his own egg is at Summerhall, that it was put in his cradle, describes the last dragon's clutch of five and the Dragonstone eggs, and his brothers' eggs (Aerion's gold-and-silver; his own white and green and swirly); Dunk, warning him to keep silent about it, reflects that he sometimes forgets Aegon is a prince.
205	   - Agent: Egg (reveals)
206	   - Patient: Dunk
207	   - Outcome: Dunk warns Egg not to mention the egg where any might hear
208	7. **Egg tells of the dragons' return** — Egg says the dragons will someday return, that his brother Daeron dreamed of it and King Aerys read it in a prophecy, and that his egg might be the one to hatch.
209	   - Agent: Egg
210	   - Patient: Dunk
211	8. **Egg recounts Fireball's history** — Egg tells Fireball's tale: master-at-arms at the Red Keep who taught Egg's father, uncles, and the Great Bastards; set aside for the Kingsguard by Daeron's naming of Willam Wylde; helped convince Daemon to claim the crown and rescued him from arrest; killed Lord Lefford at Lannisport, routed the Grey Lion, cut down Lady Penrose's sons (sparing the youngest); and was slain by a common archer before the Redgrass Field.
212	   - Agent: Egg (narrates)
213	   - Patient: Dunk
214	9. **The ferry arrives; Dunk decides on Whitewalls** — Seeing the ferry creep across the lake, Dunk resolves to go to Whitewalls to see the dragon's egg and jokes that if he wins the tourney they will both have dragon's eggs; Egg gives him a doubtful look and says he must "learn to hold my tongue."
215	   - Agent: Dunk (decides)
216	   - Location: The ferry landing
217	   - Outcome: They will go to Whitewalls
218	10. **Seating below the salt at Whitewalls** — The hedge knights are seated well below the salt; Whitewalls and its hall are described; the three uninvited knights are welcomed as it is ill luck to refuse hospitality on a wedding day.
219	    - Agent: Butterwell's household (seats them)
220	    - Patient: Dunk, Maynard, Kyle
221	    - Location: The great hall
222	11. **The dispute over Glendon at the door** — Butterwell's steward denies Glendon is Fireball's son ("Fireball never had a son"); Glendon touches his sword hilt and a dozen men-at-arms appear with spears; Kirby Pimm defuses it, clasping the steward and murmuring, laughing; the steward says something that reddens Glendon's face, and the young knight is admitted.
223	    - Agent: Butterwell's steward (denies); Ser Glendon (reacts); Kirby Pimm (intervenes)
224	    - Patient: Ser Glendon Ball
225	    - Instrument: Ser Glendon's sword hilt; the men-at-arms' spears
226	    - Witness: Dunk (watches from afar)
227	    - Outcome: Bloodshed averted; Glendon admitted to the hall
228	12. **Egg turned away to the inner yard** — When Dunk tries to bring Egg into the hall, the understeward says the great hall is for lords and knights and directs squires, grooms, and men-at-arms to tables in the inner yard; Dunk warns Egg to watch his tongue among the grown men and leaves him there.
229	    - Agent: The understeward (turns him away); Dunk (leaves Egg)
230	    - Patient: Egg
231	    - Location: The hall door / inner yard
232	    - Outcome: Egg dines in the yard
233	13. **Ser Glendon protests his seat** — Glendon tells the understeward "This cannot be my proper place" and invokes his father; the understeward tells him to take his seat or take his leave, and the boy sits below the salt, sullen.
234	    - Agent: Ser Glendon (protests); the understeward (rebuffs)
235	    - Patient: Ser Glendon Ball
236	    - Outcome: Glendon sits below the salt
237	14. **Dunk eats and is noticed** — Dunk hollows a black-bread trencher; Maynard remarks that the girls on the dais cannot take their eyes off Dunk and points out the Old Ox (Ser Buford Bulwer); Dunk, used to being stared at, says "Let them look."
238	    - Agent: Dunk; Ser Maynard (observes)
239	    - Location: Below the salt
240	15. **Ser Uthor Underleaf introduces himself and explains the tourney** — Uthor gives his name, sizes up Dunk as a formidable competitor, and lays out the field: no melee, only the joust; besides the dragon's egg, thirty golden dragons for the loser of the final tilt and ten each for those beaten the round before, plus ransoms and heavy wagering among Butterwell's guests.
241	    - Agent: Ser Uthor Underleaf
242	    - Patient: Dunk, Ser Glendon, the bench
243	    - Outcome: Dunk weighs the prizes
244	16. **The wedding procession** — Ambrose Butterwell enters to a fanfare and escorts his bride down the Myrish carpet to the dais; behind them come Lord Frey and his heir, Lords Costayne and Risley with their Butterwell-daughter wives, Frey's daughters and their husbands, Lord Gormon Peake, Lords Smallwood and Shawney, and lesser lords and knights (among them John the Fiddler and a drunken Alyn Cockshaw); the banners of Frey and Butterwell hang overhead.
245	    - Agent: Lord Butterwell (escorts the bride)
246	    - Patient: Lady Butterwell
247	    - Location: The great hall
248	17. **Lord Frey leads the toasts** — Frey toasts the king; Butterwell; the bride (with a bawdy wish for grandsons); the King's Hand, Brynden Rivers; then others toast young Lord Tully, the ailing Leo Longthorn, and the gallant dead; the Fiddler proposes the last toast, to "my brave brothers."
249	    - Agent: Lord Frey (and others)
250	    - Location: The great hall
251	18. **Glendon refuses the toast to Bloodraven** — At the toast to Brynden Rivers, Ser Glendon turns his cup over to spill it on the floor, saying he does not drink to kinslayers and calling Bloodraven a sorcerer and a bastard; Uthor answers that Bloodraven was legitimized by his royal father on his deathbed; many others lower or upturn their cups.
252	    - Agent: Ser Glendon Ball
253	    - Instrument: His wine cup (spilled)
254	    - Witness: Dunk, Maynard, Uthor
255	    - Outcome: Open display of disdain for the Hand
256	19. **The feast is served** — Butterwell declares the feast begun; the high table gets suckling pig, peacock, and pike while the below-salt guests get plainer substitutes; Dunk eats well and pockets food for Egg; talk turns to the morrow's jousting and its competitors.
257	    - Agent: Butterwell (opens the feast); Dunk (pockets food)
258	    - Location: The great hall
259	20. **John the Fiddler joins the hedge knights** — The Fiddler appears behind Dunk, boasts he is the champion, asks to join the bench (preferring hedge knights to "fat pink ladies and old men"), bids Dunk shove over, and greets Glendon as a son of Fireball; Glendon is taken aback that the Fiddler knows him.
260	    - Agent: Ser John the Fiddler
261	    - Patient: Dunk; Ser Glendon
262	    - Location: Below the salt
263	21. **The wedding pie is cut open** — Six kitchen boys wheel in the immense pie; Lord and Lady Butterwell descend and cut it open with swords, loosing half a hundred live birds of many kinds; Kyle and Maynard trade banter over its meaning.
264	    - Agent: Lord and Lady Butterwell (cut it open)
265	    - Instrument: Their swords
266	    - Location: The great hall
267	    - Outcome: Birds fly loose about the hall
268	22. **Dunk excuses himself** — Needing a piss (called "a breath of air"), Dunk shoves back from the table and excuses himself; the Fiddler tells him to hurry back, as jugglers are yet to come and he "do not want to miss the bedding."
269	    - Agent: Dunk
270	    - Location: The great hall

316	## Dialogue of Note
317	| Speaker | Listener | Quote/Paraphrase | Context |
318	|---------|----------|------------------|---------|
319	| Egg | Dunk | "We could go first if you let me use my boot." | Proposing to jump the ferry queue |
320	| Dunk | Egg | "Lord Costayne and Lord Shawney were here before us. Besides, they're lords." | Refusing |
321	| Egg | Dunk | "Rebel lords. They were for the Black Dragon." | Identifying the lords as former rebels |
322	| Dunk | Egg | "Old dead history. They're here now, aren't they? So they bent the knee, and King Daeron gave them pardon." | On the rebels' pardon |
323	| Egg | Dunk | "I'd show you mine, ser, but it's at Summerhall." | Revealing he owns a dragon's egg |
324	| Dunk | Egg | "Do you want a clout in the ear? There are no dragons." | Disbelief |
325	| Egg | Dunk | "No, but there are eggs. The last dragon left a clutch of five, and they have more on Dragonstone, old ones from before the Dance." | On the surviving eggs |
326	| Egg | Dunk | "Someday the dragons will return. My brother Daeron's dreamed of it, and King Aerys read it in a prophecy. Maybe it will be my egg that hatches." | On the dragons' return |
327	| Egg | Dunk | "Aemon and I used to pretend that our eggs would be the ones to hatch. If they did, we could fly through the sky on dragonback, like the first Aegon and his sisters." | Childhood play |
328	| Egg | Dunk | "For his hot head and red hair. Ser Quentyn Ball was the master-at-arms at the Red Keep." | Why Fireball was so called |
329	| Egg | Dunk | "An archer put an arrow through his throat as he dismounted by a stream to have a drink. Just some common man, no one knows who." | Fireball's death |
330	| Dunk | Egg | "Why not? I want to see this dragon's egg. If I win the tourney, we'd both have dragon's eggs." | Deciding to go to Whitewalls |
331	| Egg | Dunk | "I could tell you, ser, but I need to learn to hold my tongue." | Doubting Dunk's tourney chances |
332	| Butterwell's steward | Ser Glendon | "Fireball never had a son." | Denying Glendon at the door |
333	| The understeward | Egg (via Dunk) | "The great hall is for the lords and knights. We have set up tables in the inner yard for squires, grooms, and men-at-arms." | Turning Egg away |
334	| Ser Glendon | The understeward | "This cannot be my proper place. Do you know who my father was?" | Protesting his seat |
335	| The understeward | Ser Glendon | "Please take your seat or take your leave, ser. It is all the same to me." | Rebuffing Glendon |
336	| Ser Maynard | Dunk | "Ser Duncan, you appear to be attracting a deal of attention. Those girls up on the dais cannot seem to take their eyes off you." | On Dunk's size drawing stares |
337	| Ser Uthor | Dunk | "In such a field as this, your size alone should make you one of the most formidable competitors." | Sizing up Dunk |
338	| Ser Uthor | The bench | "Ser Uthor Underleaf. The son of no one of importance." | Giving his name |
339	| Ser Glendon | Dunk / the bench | "If we meet, he'll fall. I don't care how big he is." | Boasting he will beat Dunk |
340	| Dunk | The bench | "I am better with a sword than with a lance, and even better with a battleaxe. Will there be a melee here?" | On his own skills |
341	| Ser Maynard | The bench | "A marriage is a melee, as any married man could tell you." | Joking |
342	| Ser Uthor | Dunk / the bench | "Lord Butterwell has promised thirty golden dragons for the loser of the final tilt, and ten each for the knights defeated in the round before." | The prizes |
343	| Lord Frey | The hall | "Lady Butterwell, the maiden brick, my darling daughter. May the Mother make her fertile." | Toasting the bride |
344	| Lord Frey | The bride | "I shall want a grandson before the year is out. Twins would suit me even better, so churn the butter well tonight, my sweet." | Bawdy toast |
345	| Lord Frey | The hall | "I give you the King's Hand, Brynden Rivers. May the Crone's lamp light his path to wisdom." | Toasting the Hand |
346	| Ser Glendon | The bench | "I do not drink to kinslayers. Lord Bloodraven is a sorcerer and a bastard." | Refusing the toast |
347	| Ser Uthor | The bench | "Born bastard, but his royal father made him legitimate as he lay dying." | On Bloodraven's legitimation |
348	| Ser John the Fiddler | The hall (toast) | "To my brave brothers! I know that they are smiling tonight!" | The final toast |
349	| Ser John the Fiddler | Dunk / the bench | "Do not trouble to search the hall for the champion. Here I stand, sers. Feast your eyes." | Boasting |
350	| Ser John the Fiddler | Ser Glendon | "I would never wish to give offense to any son of Fireball." | Greeting Glendon |
351	| Ser Uthor | The bench | "Black Tom Heddle. A nasty bit of business, that one. He won the hand of His Lordship's eldest daughter by killing three of her other suitors, it's said, and once unhorsed the Lord of Casterly Rock." | On the favorite |
352	| Ser Kyle | The bench | "The pie is meant to be the marriage, and a true marriage has in it many sorts of things. . .joy and grief, pain and pleasure, love and lust and loyalty." | On the wedding pie |
353	| Ser John the Fiddler | Dunk | "Flurry back, ser. There are jugglers yet to come, and you do not want to miss the bedding." | As Dunk leaves |
354	
355	## POV Character's Internal State
356	- **Emotional state:** Hot and impatient at the ferry; wary of Ser Maynard Plumm; amused and quietly astonished by Egg's revelation that he owns a dragon's egg; anxious in the crowded hall about being recognized ("If I am recognized . . ."); uncomfortable under the dais girls' stares; tempted by the tourney prizes; grows a thirst and drinks more than he meant to.
357	- **Primary preoccupation:** Seeing the dragon's egg (and perhaps winning it and the ten-dragon prize); keeping Egg quiet and his own and Egg's identities hidden; getting fed.
358	- **Key decisions made:** To wait his turn at the ferry rather than let Egg "use his boot"; to silence Egg over the rebel lords and the dragon egg; to go to Whitewalls to see the egg; to consider entering the joust; to pocket food for Egg; to excuse himself from the table.
359	- **Self-deception flags:** Tells himself "It would have been discourteous not to toast the bride and groom . . . and dangerous not to drink to the king and his Hand" — rationalizing drinking too much before the jousting; jokes "If I win the tourney, we'd both have dragon's eggs" though he admits he is poor with a lance (and Egg gives him a doubtful look).

# TMK — Dunk 1

## Chapter Metadata
- **book:** TMK
- **chapter_number:** 1
- **pov_character:** Dunk
- **pov_chapter_number:** Dunk I
- **first_available:** pre-agot
- **location_primary:** Whitewalls (Lord Butterwell's castle — the great hall and the wedding feast)
- **location_secondary:** The ferry landing and the lake crossing; the inner yard of Whitewalls (squires' tables); (mentioned) Summerhall, Dragonstone, the Red Keep, Lannisport, Casterly Rock/the Rock, the Mandel, the Redgrass Field, Ashford Meadow, the Vale, the Green Fork, Flag's Mire, Crackclaw Point, Highgarden
- **approximate_timeline:** "The next day"; unit part 3 of 9. Morning at the ferry crossing gives way to the wedding feast at Whitewalls that same evening. Sequence: the slow ferry crossing and Egg's talk of dragon eggs and Fireball; arrival and seating below the salt at Whitewalls; the wedding procession and toasts; the feast, the tourney talk, the arrival of John the Fiddler, and the wedding pie.
- **time_markers:** "The next day"; "each taking more than an hour"; "it would be midday before they were allowed to board the ferry"; Whitewalls "having been raised a mere forty years ago by the grandsire of its present lord"; "The girl was fifteen and freshly flowered, her lord husband fifty and freshly widowed"; "his heir a chinless boy of four"; Frey wants a grandson "before the year is out"; "died in the spring" (the Great Spring Sickness); Bulwer "Past sixty"; "the jousting on the morrow"
- **chapter_summary:** The next day Dunk and Egg wait through the slow ferry crossing while Lords Costayne and Shawney quarrel over precedence and the Smallwoods arrive; as they wait, Egg reveals he owns a dragon's egg kept at Summerhall, describes his brothers' eggs and a prophecy that the dragons will return, and recounts the history of Ser Quentyn Ball, called Fireball. Deciding to see the dragon's egg for himself, Dunk brings them to Whitewalls, Lord Butterwell's white-stone castle, where the uninvited hedge knights are seated below the salt for the wedding feast and Egg is turned away to the inner yard. At the bench Dunk meets Ser Uthor Underleaf and learns of the tourney's prizes — the dragon's egg and thirty golden dragons for the loser of the final tilt. Lord Ambrose Butterwell weds his fifteen-year-old Frey bride amid a procession of lords and a round of toasts, during which Ser Glendon Ball spills his cup rather than drink to the Hand, Brynden Rivers. Over the feast the talk turns to tourney competitors; John the Fiddler appears and joins the hedge knights, and a great wedding pie is cut open to loose a hall full of live birds before Dunk excuses himself.
- **unit_part:** 3 of 9

## Physical Environment
- **Weather:** Hot and sweltering at the ferry landing ("nought that Dunk could do but wait and swelter"); midge bites on Dunk's neck
- **Season indicators:** Heat and midges; "died in the spring" (the Great Spring Sickness, recalled); the bride "freshly flowered"
- **Time of day:** Morning to midday at the ferry crossing; evening for the wedding feast (the bedding to come after)
- **Lighting:** The great hall lit for the feast; Butterwell prefers his lord's box "in the shade"; amethysts on the Fiddler's chain "dark"; the pale white marble and gold-veined interior
- **Sounds:** Costayne and Shawney's "shouting match over precedence"; a fanfare of trumpets from the minstrel's gallery; "pipes and fiddles filled the air with spritely tunes"; "Laughter rang against the rafters"; squeaks, squawks, and thumps from inside the wedding pie; half a hundred birds bursting forth
- **Smells:** The Fiddler "smelled of oranges and limes, with a hint of some strange eastern spice beneath" (nutmeg, perhaps); the ripe white cheese that "smelled as strong as Bennis of the Brown Shield"
- **Notable sensory details:** Whitewalls of finely dressed white stone with floors and pillars of milky white marble veined with gold and rafters carved from bone-pale weirwood trunks; the patterned Myrish carpet; the double throne of gilded oak; the two great banners hung from the rafters; the black-bread trencher; the immense brown wedding pie

## Events & Actions
1. **The ferry crossing** — Ned's ferry is too small for all who wish to cross, so Lords Costayne and Shawney go first with their tails; each of several trips takes more than an hour, slowed by mudflats and by the two lords' shouting match over precedence (Shawney elder, Costayne better born).
   - Agent: Ned (ferryman, implied); Lords Costayne and Shawney (cross first)
   - Location: The ferry landing and lake
   - Outcome: The hedge knights must wait
2. **Egg offers to use his boot to go first** — Egg says they could go first "if you let me use my boot"; Dunk refuses, because the lords were there first and are lords.
   - Agent: Egg (proposes); Dunk (refuses)
   - Patient: Dunk
   - Outcome: Refused; the party waits their turn
3. **Egg names Costayne and Shawney as former rebels** — Egg says they were "for the Black Dragon" (Shawney, and Costayne's father), describing Costayne's arms and the placement of their banners in Daemon's host; Dunk pinches the boy's lips shut and tells him to hold his tongue.
   - Agent: Egg (speaks); Dunk (silences)
   - Patient: Egg
   - Instrument: Dunk pinching Egg's lips
   - Outcome: Egg held his tongue
4. **The Smallwoods arrive; the party waits again** — Lord and Lady Smallwood arrive at the landing with their tail, so the hedge knights must wait once more; the "fellowship of the hedge" has dissolved (Glendon aloof, Kyle courting Smallwood, Maynard gossiping with the innkeep).
   - Agent: Lord and Lady Smallwood
   - Location: The ferry landing
   - Outcome: Further delay
5. **Dunk warns Egg away from Ser Maynard Plumm** — Something about Plumm troubles Dunk; he warns Egg to stay well away, saying "He could be a robber knight"; the warning only makes Plumm more interesting to Egg.
   - Agent: Dunk
   - Patient: Egg
6. **Egg reveals he owns a dragon's egg** — Egg says his own egg is at Summerhall, that it was put in his cradle, describes the last dragon's clutch of five and the Dragonstone eggs, and his brothers' eggs (Aerion's gold-and-silver; his own white and green and swirly); Dunk, warning him to keep silent about it, reflects that he sometimes forgets Aegon is a prince.
   - Agent: Egg (reveals)
   - Patient: Dunk
   - Outcome: Dunk warns Egg not to mention the egg where any might hear
7. **Egg tells of the dragons' return** — Egg says the dragons will someday return, that his brother Daeron dreamed of it and King Aerys read it in a prophecy, and that his egg might be the one to hatch.
   - Agent: Egg
   - Patient: Dunk
8. **Egg recounts Fireball's history** — Egg tells Fireball's tale: master-at-arms at the Red Keep who taught Egg's father, uncles, and the Great Bastards; set aside for the Kingsguard by Daeron's naming of Willam Wylde; helped convince Daemon to claim the crown and rescued him from arrest; killed Lord Lefford at Lannisport, routed the Grey Lion, cut down Lady Penrose's sons (sparing the youngest); and was slain by a common archer before the Redgrass Field.
   - Agent: Egg (narrates)
   - Patient: Dunk
9. **The ferry arrives; Dunk decides on Whitewalls** — Seeing the ferry creep across the lake, Dunk resolves to go to Whitewalls to see the dragon's egg and jokes that if he wins the tourney they will both have dragon's eggs; Egg gives him a doubtful look and says he must "learn to hold my tongue."
   - Agent: Dunk (decides)
   - Location: The ferry landing
   - Outcome: They will go to Whitewalls
10. **Seating below the salt at Whitewalls** — The hedge knights are seated well below the salt; Whitewalls and its hall are described; the three uninvited knights are welcomed as it is ill luck to refuse hospitality on a wedding day.
    - Agent: Butterwell's household (seats them)
    - Patient: Dunk, Maynard, Kyle
    - Location: The great hall
11. **The dispute over Glendon at the door** — Butterwell's steward denies Glendon is Fireball's son ("Fireball never had a son"); Glendon touches his sword hilt and a dozen men-at-arms appear with spears; Kirby Pimm defuses it, clasping the steward and murmuring, laughing; the steward says something that reddens Glendon's face, and the young knight is admitted.
    - Agent: Butterwell's steward (denies); Ser Glendon (reacts); Kirby Pimm (intervenes)
    - Patient: Ser Glendon Ball
    - Instrument: Ser Glendon's sword hilt; the men-at-arms' spears
    - Witness: Dunk (watches from afar)
    - Outcome: Bloodshed averted; Glendon admitted to the hall
12. **Egg turned away to the inner yard** — When Dunk tries to bring Egg into the hall, the understeward says the great hall is for lords and knights and directs squires, grooms, and men-at-arms to tables in the inner yard; Dunk warns Egg to watch his tongue among the grown men and leaves him there.
    - Agent: The understeward (turns him away); Dunk (leaves Egg)
    - Patient: Egg
    - Location: The hall door / inner yard
    - Outcome: Egg dines in the yard
13. **Ser Glendon protests his seat** — Glendon tells the understeward "This cannot be my proper place" and invokes his father; the understeward tells him to take his seat or take his leave, and the boy sits below the salt, sullen.
    - Agent: Ser Glendon (protests); the understeward (rebuffs)
    - Patient: Ser Glendon Ball
    - Outcome: Glendon sits below the salt
14. **Dunk eats and is noticed** — Dunk hollows a black-bread trencher; Maynard remarks that the girls on the dais cannot take their eyes off Dunk and points out the Old Ox (Ser Buford Bulwer); Dunk, used to being stared at, says "Let them look."
    - Agent: Dunk; Ser Maynard (observes)
    - Location: Below the salt
15. **Ser Uthor Underleaf introduces himself and explains the tourney** — Uthor gives his name, sizes up Dunk as a formidable competitor, and lays out the field: no melee, only the joust; besides the dragon's egg, thirty golden dragons for the loser of the final tilt and ten each for those beaten the round before, plus ransoms and heavy wagering among Butterwell's guests.
    - Agent: Ser Uthor Underleaf
    - Patient: Dunk, Ser Glendon, the bench
    - Outcome: Dunk weighs the prizes
16. **The wedding procession** — Ambrose Butterwell enters to a fanfare and escorts his bride down the Myrish carpet to the dais; behind them come Lord Frey and his heir, Lords Costayne and Risley with their Butterwell-daughter wives, Frey's daughters and their husbands, Lord Gormon Peake, Lords Smallwood and Shawney, and lesser lords and knights (among them John the Fiddler and a drunken Alyn Cockshaw); the banners of Frey and Butterwell hang overhead.
    - Agent: Lord Butterwell (escorts the bride)
    - Patient: Lady Butterwell
    - Location: The great hall
17. **Lord Frey leads the toasts** — Frey toasts the king; Butterwell; the bride (with a bawdy wish for grandsons); the King's Hand, Brynden Rivers; then others toast young Lord Tully, the ailing Leo Longthorn, and the gallant dead; the Fiddler proposes the last toast, to "my brave brothers."
    - Agent: Lord Frey (and others)
    - Location: The great hall
18. **Glendon refuses the toast to Bloodraven** — At the toast to Brynden Rivers, Ser Glendon turns his cup over to spill it on the floor, saying he does not drink to kinslayers and calling Bloodraven a sorcerer and a bastard; Uthor answers that Bloodraven was legitimized by his royal father on his deathbed; many others lower or upturn their cups.
    - Agent: Ser Glendon Ball
    - Instrument: His wine cup (spilled)
    - Witness: Dunk, Maynard, Uthor
    - Outcome: Open display of disdain for the Hand
19. **The feast is served** — Butterwell declares the feast begun; the high table gets suckling pig, peacock, and pike while the below-salt guests get plainer substitutes; Dunk eats well and pockets food for Egg; talk turns to the morrow's jousting and its competitors.
    - Agent: Butterwell (opens the feast); Dunk (pockets food)
    - Location: The great hall
20. **John the Fiddler joins the hedge knights** — The Fiddler appears behind Dunk, boasts he is the champion, asks to join the bench (preferring hedge knights to "fat pink ladies and old men"), bids Dunk shove over, and greets Glendon as a son of Fireball; Glendon is taken aback that the Fiddler knows him.
    - Agent: Ser John the Fiddler
    - Patient: Dunk; Ser Glendon
    - Location: Below the salt
21. **The wedding pie is cut open** — Six kitchen boys wheel in the immense pie; Lord and Lady Butterwell descend and cut it open with swords, loosing half a hundred live birds of many kinds; Kyle and Maynard trade banter over its meaning.
    - Agent: Lord and Lady Butterwell (cut it open)
    - Instrument: Their swords
    - Location: The great hall
    - Outcome: Birds fly loose about the hall
22. **Dunk excuses himself** — Needing a piss (called "a breath of air"), Dunk shoves back from the table and excuses himself; the Fiddler tells him to hurry back, as jugglers are yet to come and he "do not want to miss the bedding."
    - Agent: Dunk
    - Location: The great hall

| Dunk | Egg | "Do you want a clout in the ear? There are no dragons." | Disbelief |

## POV Character's Internal State
- **Emotional state:** Hot and impatient at the ferry; wary of Ser Maynard Plumm; amused and quietly astonished by Egg's revelation that he owns a dragon's egg; anxious in the crowded hall about being recognized ("If I am recognized . . ."); uncomfortable under the dais girls' stares; tempted by the tourney prizes; grows a thirst and drinks more than he meant to.
- **Primary preoccupation:** Seeing the dragon's egg (and perhaps winning it and the ten-dragon prize); keeping Egg quiet and his own and Egg's identities hidden; getting fed.
- **Key decisions made:** To wait his turn at the ferry rather than let Egg "use his boot"; to silence Egg over the rebel lords and the dragon egg; to go to Whitewalls to see the egg; to consider entering the joust; to pocket food for Egg; to excuse himself from the table.
- **Self-deception flags:** Tells himself "It would have been discourteous not to toast the bride and groom . . . and dangerous not to drink to the king and his Hand" — rationalizing drinking too much before the jousting; jokes "If I win the tourney, we'd both have dragon's eggs" though he admits he is poor with a lance (and Egg gives him a doubtful look).

- **pov_chapter_number:** Dunk I
- **first_available:** pre-agot
- **location_primary:** Whitewalls (Lord Butterwell's castle — the outer yard, the great hall, the bedchamber, the tower roof, and the pavilion field below the western walls)
- **location_secondary:** The kennels, the sept, and the stone steps of Whitewalls; Dunk's tent below the western walls; (mentioned) Coldmoat Castle, the Twins, Ashford Meadow, the Neck, Winterfell, the kingsroad, the Wall, the Three Sisters / Sisterton / the Bite, Stoney Sept, Harrenhal
- **approximate_timeline:** The wedding night, continuing directly from the feast; unit part 4 of 9. Sequence: Dunk leaves the hall to relieve himself and overhears Peake and another lord conspiring; returns to the frolics; the bedding, in which he is made to carry the bride; he sees the dragon's egg and is warned off it; he retreats to the tower roof where the Fiddler reveals his ambitions; Peake threatens him; he returns to his tent and instructs Egg to enter him in the lists under a false name.
- **time_markers:** "Outside, the night wind"; the jousting "on the morrow"; "It might be a year before I have another" (cup of hippocras); the Black Dragon's cause "Sixteen years ago"; the Fiddler "They were twelve and I was only seven... I am two-and-twenty now"; night, "beneath the stars," "moonlight"
- **chapter_summary:** Drunk and seeking a privy in the outer yard of Whitewalls, Dunk overhears Lord Gormon Peake and a deep-voiced lord conspiring over a prince, a dragon's egg they expect to hatch, Bittersteel, Bloodraven, and the gold and swords they mean to gather. Back inside he endures the wedding frolics, hears Ser Maynard Plumm's tale that the bride was deflowered by a scullion at the Twins, and is dragooned into carrying the bride to the marriage bed during the bedding, where he handles Lord Butterwell's dragon's egg and is warned off it by the black-bearded knight from Peake's conversation. Retreating to the tower roof, Dunk is joined by John the Fiddler, who claims to have dreamed Dunk as a Kingsguard knight, declares he will take the throne, and insists a dragon will hatch and that he must have the egg. Peake and two guards remove the Fiddler to bed and Peake threatens Dunk with death should he repeat what he heard, whereupon Dunk vomits on his boots. Returning to his tent, Dunk finds Egg reading a roll of arms with a split lip from fighting squires who called his father, Prince Maekar, a kinslayer; Dunk tells him to hold back his true name and enter him in the lists as the Gallows Knight.
- **unit_part:** 4 of 9

## Physical Environment
- **Weather:** Night wind ("the night wind lapped at Dunk like the tongue of some great beast"); the ground seems to move beneath Dunk's feet (drunkenness); Dunk's tent "kept the rain and sun off"
- **Season indicators:** Warm enough for open-air pavilions and rooftop lingering at night; (mentioned) the north where "it snows there even in the summer"
- **Time of day:** Night — the wedding night; "beneath the stars," "the pale castle glimmering in the moonlight"; guests still awake late, pavilions "glowing like colored lanterns in the night"
- **Lighting:** Moonlight and starlight on the white castle; the bedchamber lit by "a hundred scented candles" in nooks and crannies and by lamps beside the dragon's egg; Egg reads by a single candle; the silken pavilion walls glow "like colored lanterns"
- **Sounds:** Hounds barking and howling at the kennels; a harp playing "Two Hearts That Beat As One" badly; the singing of "The Bear and the Maiden Fair" ("A bear! A bear! All black and brown, and covered with hair!"); pig's-bladders making "rude noises"; Frey's son's "high shrill hiccup of a laugh"; the shouts of "Bed them! Bed them!"; ribald japes; laughter and "sounds of love" from the pavilions
- **Smells:** The whitewash on the tilting barrier; the scented candles in the bedchamber
- **Notable sensory details:** The dragon's egg — fine red scales "shining bright as jewels," heavier than expected, with gold flecks and "whorls of midnight black"; the suit of gem-and-gold-inlaid plate by the bedchamber door; the Myrish carpets; the bride nearly naked "save for the stocking on her left leg"

## Events & Actions
1. **Dunk leaves the hall drunk and crosses the outer yard** — Swaying with wine, Dunk goes out to find the inner ward and send Egg to enter him in the lists; he passes the erected lists, the three-tiered viewing stand, and the tents with their lance-racks.
   - Agent: Dunk
   - Location: The outer yard
   - Outcome: Dunk gets turned around in the unfamiliar castle
2. **Dunk gets lost, passing the kennels and the sept** — The hounds bark and howl at him; he doubles back past the sept, where a laughing woman runs by pursued by a falling-down bald knight; needing to relieve himself, he steps behind bushes beneath a flight of pale stone steps.
   - Agent: Dunk
   - Location: The kennels, sept, and stone steps
   - Outcome: Dunk relieves himself behind the bushes
3. **Dunk overhears Peake and the black-bearded knight conspiring** — From above the steps, Dunk hears Lord Peake and a deep-voiced lord discuss Bittersteel ("No bastard can be trusted"), a prince who insists the egg will hatch and once dreamed his brothers dead, Old Milkblood's expectations, Bloodraven, and their plan to gather Butterwell's gold and House Frey's swords so that Harrenhal and then the Brackens will follow. Dunk holds his breath and does not reveal himself.
   - Agent: Lord Gormon Peake; the black-bearded knight (speak)
   - Patient: Dunk (unseen listener)
   - Witness: Dunk (overhears from concealment)
   - Location: Below the stone steps
   - Outcome: Dunk keeps hidden; wonders whom they mean ("Fireball's son?")
4. **The wedding frolics** — Back in the hall Dunk finds a Frey daughter playing the harp badly, jugglers and tumblers, and the hall bellowing "The Bear and the Maiden Fair"; Lord Caswell passes out face-down in wine and Lady Vyrwel weeps for an unknown cause; the wine keeps flowing.
   - Agent: The entertainers and guests
   - Location: The great hall
5. **Maynard tells the tale of the bride's scullion** — Pointing out Frey's chinless son as "the lad who made this marriage," Ser Maynard says the bride was deflowered by a scullion at the Twins and caught with him by her little brother, forcing the match; Dunk doubts it, reasoning Butterwell would not wed and gift a dragon's egg for a soiled girl.
   - Agent: Ser Maynard Plumm
   - Patient: Dunk (listener)
   - Location: The great hall
6. **Dunk dozes and dreams** — He lays his head on his arms and sleeps, dreaming of Tanselle Too-Tall and the Red Widow, until the shouts of "Bed them!" wake him.
   - Agent: Dunk
   - Location: The great hall
7. **The bedding begins** — Half the guests are on their feet shouting; Ser Franklyn Frey carries the bride down the aisle while men strip her, and the ladies pull Lord Butterwell from his chair, unlacing his boots and tunic as he flails and laughs, both groom and Frey drunk.
   - Agent: Ser Franklyn Frey; Lady Vyrwel and the ladies
   - Patient: The bride; Lord Butterwell
   - Location: The great hall
8. **Dunk is made to carry the bride** — John the Fiddler drags Dunk to his feet crying "Let the giant carry her!"; Dunk climbs the tower stair with the squirming, near-naked bride in his arms, the men japing and the dwarfs swarming his legs with bladders.
   - Agent: Ser John the Fiddler (instigates); Dunk (carries)
   - Patient: The bride
   - Instigator: Ser John the Fiddler
   - Location: The tower stair
   - Outcome: Dunk reaches the bedchamber; the bride down to one stocking
9. **Dunk deposits the bride and hauls off a groping dwarf** — In the lavish bedchamber Dunk plops the bride on the marriage bed; a dwarf leaps beside her and seizes her breast; Dunk grabs the dwarf by the collar to throw him out.
   - Agent: Dunk
   - Patient: The dwarf; the bride
   - Location: The bedchamber
10. **Dunk handles the dragon's egg** — Seeing the egg on its plinth, Dunk drops the dwarf and lifts the egg — bigger than a hen's egg, red-scaled with gold flecks and black whorls, heavier than expected — turning it in his hands.
    - Agent: Dunk
    - Patient: The dragon's egg
    - Location: The bedchamber
11. **The black-bearded knight warns Dunk off the egg** — The big black-bearded, boil-marked knight — whose deep angry voice Dunk recognizes as Peake's companion — orders him to put the egg down and keep his "greasy fingers off His Lordship's treasures"; Dunk complies, sets the egg back carefully, and shoves past him out the door.
    - Agent: The black-bearded knight (warns); Dunk (complies)
    - Patient: Dunk
    - Location: The bedchamber
    - Outcome: Dunk leaves; Dunk realizes the knight is the man from Peake's conversation
12. **Dunk retreats to the tower roof** — To avoid the women bringing Butterwell to his bride, Dunk climbs to the tower roof beneath the stars; feeling sick and guilty, he recalls Tanselle's puppet show and the three men dead at Ashford.
    - Agent: Dunk
    - Location: The tower roof
13. **The Fiddler reveals his dreams and ambition** — John the Fiddler joins Dunk and says he dreamed of Dunk as a White Sword of the Kingsguard, that his dreams are true, that he will "take the throne" to make Dunk a Kingsguard, and that a dragon will hatch from the egg; he recounts dreaming his brothers dead when he was seven and they twelve. He asks for Dunk's sword and says he must have the dragon's egg.
    - Agent: Ser John the Fiddler (reveals)
    - Patient: Dunk
    - Location: The tower roof
    - Outcome: Dunk is unconvinced, remembering Daeron's dream and Baelor's death
14. **Peake removes the Fiddler and threatens Dunk** — Peake and two men-at-arms come onto the roof; Peake's guards take the Fiddler down to bed, reminding him he jousts Kirby Pimm on the morrow. Alone with Dunk, Peake asks what the Fiddler promised, then threatens "three feet of cold steel through your belly" if Dunk repeats what he heard.
    - Agent: Lord Gormon Peake (threatens); the guards (remove the Fiddler)
    - Patient: Dunk; Ser John the Fiddler
    - Instigator: Lord Gormon Peake (commands the guards)
    - Location: The tower roof
    - Outcome: The Fiddler is taken to bed; Dunk is threatened to silence
15. **Dunk vomits on Peake's boots** — Trying to clear his wits, Dunk bends double and retches, spattering Peake's boots; Peake curses hedge knights, and Dunk answers "We are wanted nowhere and turn up everywhere"; Peake repeats his warning and departs.
    - Agent: Dunk
    - Patient: Lord Gormon Peake (boots)
    - Location: The tower roof
16. **Dunk returns to the hall, then his tent** — Only Ser Maynard remains, asking whether the bride had flour on her; Dunk pours and abandons a last cup of wine, then crosses the pavilion field to his tent, where Egg sits reading a roll of arms by candlelight.
    - Agent: Dunk
    - Location: The hall; the pavilion field; the tent
17. **Egg's split lip and the traitor's tourney** — Dunk sees Egg's broken lip; Egg admits he fought squires who called Prince Maekar a kinslayer, hitting Ser Mallor's squire after a jibe about Aerys. Egg points out that Lord Sunderland — a Sisterman bearing three pale ladies' heads on undy green and blue — fought for the Black Dragon, and calls it "a traitor's tourney."
    - Agent: Egg (reveals); the squires (insulted Maekar, earlier/recalled)
    - Patient: Egg
    - Location: The tent
    - Outcome: Dunk chides Egg for not holding his tongue
18. **Dunk orders the false name** — Dunk tells Egg to find Cosgrove, the master of the games, and enter him not under his own name but as "the Gallows Knight," lest a lord recall Ser Duncan the Tall from Ashford; he pinches out the candle.
    - Agent: Dunk
    - Patient: Egg
    - Location: The tent
    - Outcome: Dunk will compete as a mystery knight

## POV Character's Internal State
- **Emotional state:** Very drunk and dizzy throughout; wary and unsettled by the overheard plot; melancholy over Tanselle and Lady Rohanne; aroused and abashed carrying the bride; guilty at touching the dragon's egg (recalling the deaths at Ashford); intimidated but defiant with Peake; sickened to vomiting; then near-sober and angry over the "traitor's tourney" and Egg's safety.
- **Primary preoccupation:** Keeping his own and Egg's identities hidden; the dragon's egg and the conspiracy around it; Egg's welfare and loose tongue; longing for Tanselle.
- **Key decisions made:** Not to shout after the conspirators or reveal himself; to put the egg back and leave the bedchamber; to go up to the roof rather than face the bedding party; to rebuff the Fiddler's offers and leave; to hold back his true name and enter the lists as "the Gallows Knight."
- **Self-deception flags:** Talks himself out of the plot's significance — "Dunk the drunk, what is it that you think you heard?"; tells Egg "Words are wind... Just let them blow on past you," minimizing the treason talk even as he grows angry and admits it is "a traitor's tourney"; dismisses his own arousal and his longing for Tanselle as things he "must have dreamed."
